elements.sent-2411-001: Delle Rose et al.~(COLT'23) introduced an effective version of the Vapnik-Chervonenkis dimension, and showed that it characterizes improper PAC learning with total computable learners.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this paper, we introduce and study a similar effectivization of the notion of Littlestone dimension.
elements.sent-2411-003: Finite effective Littlestone dimension is a necessary condition for computable online learning but is not a sufficient one---which we already establish for classes of the effective Littlestone dimension 2.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, the effective Littlestone dimension equals the optimal mistake bound for computable learners in two special cases: a) for classes of Littlestone dimension 1 and b) when the learner receives as additional information an upper bound on the numbers to be guessed.
elements.sent-2411-005: Interestingly, finite effective Littlestone dimension also guarantees that the class consists only of computable functions.
elements.sent-2411-006: Two fundamental models of machine learning, PAC learning and online learning, have been recently revisited from the viewpoint of computability theory~\cite{agarwal2020learnability,sterkenburg2022characterizations,delle2023find,hasrati2023computable .}
elements.sent-2411-007: In the classical setting, a learning algorithm is understood as a function, getting a sample $S$ and an input $x$ and outputting its prediction of the value on $x$.
elements.sent-2411-008: Although this is called an ``algorithm'', it is not assumed to have a Turing machine that computes it.
elements.sent-2411-009: The existence of a learning algorithm for a hypothesis class can be characterized by a combinatorial dimension of that class, namely, the VC dimension in the case of PAC learning and the Littlestone dimension in the case of online learning.
elements.sent-2411-010: What if we do require a learning algorithm to be computable by a Turing machine? We obtain ``computable counterparts'' of PAC and online learning models that might no longer be characterized just by a combinatorial dimension.
elements.sent-2411-011: For instance, Strekenburg~\cite{sterkenburg2022characterizations constructs a class with finite VC dimension, given by a decidable set of functions with finite support, that has no computable PAC learner, even if the learner is allowed to be improper (output functions outside the class).}
elements.sent-2411-012: Likewise, Hasrati and Ben-David~\cite{hasrati2023computable observe that there is a class that has Littlestone dimension 1 and consists of finitely supported functions but does not have an online learner, computable by a partial Turing machine (the machine must be defined on realizable samples but otherwise it might not halt) with finite number of mistakes.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Delle Rose at al.~\cite{delle2023find recently characterized computable PAC learning via an effectivization of the notion of the VC dimension.}
elements.sent-2411-014: The usual VC dimension of a class $H$ is defined as the maximal size of a subset of the domain where functions from $H$ can realize all dichotomies.
elements.sent-2411-015: The ``dual'' defintion is the minimal $d$ such that for any subset of size $d+1$ there exists a dichotomy, not realizable by $H$.
elements.sent-2411-016: In the effective version of VC dimension, there must be a Turing machine that, given an $(d+1)$-size subset, outputs a dichotomy, not realizable by $H$.
elements.sent-2411-017: The minimal $d$ for which such a Turing machine exists is called the effective VC dimension of $H$.
elements.sent-2411-018: As Delle Rose et al.~\cite{delle2023find show, classes admitting a computable PAC learner are exactly classes having finite effective VC dimension.}
elements.sent-2411-019: They asume that the learner can be improper but has to be computed by a total Turing machine, that is, it must halt even on non-realizable inputs.
elements.sent-2411-020: In this paper, we introduce a similar ``effectivization'' of the Littlestone dimension and study its relationship with the computable online learning.
elements.sent-2411-021: The usual Littlestone dimension of a hypothesis class $H$ is defined as the maximal $d$ for which there exists a depth-$d$ Littlestone tree with every branch realizable by $H$.
elements.sent-2411-022: Following the idea of~\cite{delle2023find , we define the effective Littlestone dimension of $H$ as the minimal $d$ for which there exists a Turing machine that, given a Littlestone tree of depth $d+1$, indicates a branch, not realizable by $H$.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Our contribution with respect to the effective Littlestone dimension consists of the following.
elements.sent-2411-024: In a similar manner, we define the notion of the effective threshold dimension and observe that classes with finite effective Littlestone dimension coincide with classes of finite effective threshold dimension.
elements.sent-2411-025: We observe that a class that admits an online learner, computable by a total Turing machine (that we all, for brevity, a total computable online learner), that makes at most $d$ mistakes, has effective Littlestone dimension at most $d$.
elements.sent-2411-026: We show that the converse does not hold.
elements.sent-2411-027: We construct a class of effective Littlestone dimension 2 that does not admit even a partial computable online learner (``partial'' means that the Turing machine, computing it, might not halt on some non-realizable samples) with a finite number of mistakes.
elements.sent-2411-028: On the positive side, we show that effective Littlestone dimension is equivalent to a computable online learning ``with an upper bound''.
elements.sent-2411-029: In this setting, the learner is given in an advance an upper bound on numbers it will see in the game.
elements.sent-2411-030: We also show that every class of finite effective Littlestone dimension consists of computable functions.
elements.sent-2411-031: As a consequence, every class of effective Littlestone dimension 1 admits a total computable online learner with 1 mistake.
elements.sent-2411-032: Similar failure of the combinatorial characterization of computable learning was recently observed by Gourdeau, Tosca, and Urner~\cite{gourdeau2024computability for computable robust PAC learning.}
elements.sent-2411-033: By hypothesis classes we mean sets of functions from $\mathbb{N}$ to $\{0,1\}$.
elements.sent-2411-034: By samples we mean finite sequences of pairs from $\mathbb{N}\times \{0, 1\}$.
elements.sent-2411-035: A sample $S = (x_1, y_1)\ldots (x_k, y_k)$ is consistent with a function $f\colon\mathbb{N}\to\{0,1\}$ if $f(x_1) = y_1, \ldots, f(x_k) = y_k$.
elements.sent-2411-036: A sample $S = (x_1, y_1) \ldots (x_k, y_k)$ is realizable by a hypothesis class $H$ (or $H$-realizable, for brevity) if there is a function in $H$ with which $S$ is consistent.
elements.sent-2411-037: A learner is a partial function $L\colon (\mathbb{N}\times\{0, 1\})^*\times\mathbb{N}\to\{0,1\}$ (thus, the first input to $L$ is a sample and the second input is a natural number).
elements.sent-2411-038: We say that a learner $L$ is a learner for a hypothesis class $H$ if for every $H$-realizable sample $S$ the value $L(S, x)$ is defined for every $x\in\mathbb{N}$.
elements.sent-2411-039: A total learner is a learner which is defined everywhere.
elements.sent-2411-040: Sometimes we write ``partial learner'' to stress that a statement holds not only for total learners.
elements.sent-2411-041: A learner $L$ is computable if there exists a Turing machine that outputs $L(S, x)$ on $(S, x)$ for which $L$ is defined, and does not halt on $(S, x)$ for which $L$ is not defined.
elements.sent-2411-042: For a given sample $S$, the learner induces a (possibly, partial) function $L_S\colon\mathbb{N}\to\{0,1\}$ by setting $L_S(x) = L(S, x)$, to which we refer as the hypothesis of $L$ after the sample $S$.
elements.sent-2411-043: Let $L$ be a learner and $S = (x_1, y_1)\ldots (x_k, y_k)$ be a sample.
elements.sent-2411-044: The number of mistakes of $L$ on $S$ is the number of $i\in\{1, \ldots, k \}$ such that $L((x_1, y_1)\ldots (x_{i-1 , y_{i-1 ), x_{i ) \neq y_{i}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: One can interpret this quantity as follows.
elements.sent-2411-046: Imagine that $L$ receives pairs of $S$ one by one.
elements.sent-2411-047: Each pair $(x_{i , y_{i )$ is given like this: first $L$ receives $x_{i}$ and is asked to predict $y_{i}$, using its knowledge of the preceding pairs in the sample.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: After $L$ makes a prediction, the true value of $y_{i}$ is revealed, causing a mistake if the prediction differs from $y_{i}$.
elements.sent-2411-049: A learner $L$ for a hypothesis class $H$ is called an online learner for $H$ with at most $d$ mistakes if $L$ makes at most $d$ mistakes on any $H$-realizable sample.
elements.sent-2411-050: Then every function $f\in H$ coincides with $L_S$ for some sample $S$, consistent with $f$.. \end{lem.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Indeed, if there is no such sample, we can construct a sample, consistent with $f$, on which $L$ makes more than $d$ mistakes.
elements.sent-2411-052: Namely, we start with the hypothesis of $L$ after the empty sample.
elements.sent-2411-053: It disagrees with $f$ on some $x_1\in \mathbb{N}$ which we put to the sample as $(x_1, f(x_1))$, causing the first mistake.
elements.sent-2411-054: The hypothesis of $L$ after $(x_1, f(x_1))$ disagrees with $f$ on some $x_2$, and we add this $(x_2, f(x_2))$ to the sample, forcing the second mistake, and so on.
elements.sent-2411-055: In this way, we can force arbitrarily many mistakes.
elements.sent-2411-056: Then all functions in $H$ are computable.. \end{cor.}
elements.sent-2411-057: By Lemma \ref{lemma_cons , every function $f\in H$ coincides with $L_S$ for some sample $H$-realizable sample $S$.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Since $L$ is a learner for $H$ and is computable, the function $L_S$ is computable.
elements.sent-2411-059: By a Littlestone tree of depth $d$ we mean a complete rooted binary tree of depth $d$ where: (a) edges are directed from parents to children, with each edge labeled by 0 or 1 such that every non-leaf node has one out-going 0-edge and one out-going 1-edge; and (b) non-leaf nodes are labeled by natural numbers.
elements.sent-2411-060: Every edge in such a tree can be assigned a pair $(x, y)\in\mathbb{N}\times \{0,1\}$ where $x$ is the natural number, labelling node this edge starts at, and $y$ is the bit, labelling this edge.
elements.sent-2411-061: Thus, every directed path in this tree can be assigned a sample, obtained by concatenating pairs, assigned to its edges.
elements.sent-2411-062: Now, for a vertex $v$ of a Littlestone tree $T$, and for a hypothesis class $H$, we say that $v$ is $H$-realizable if the sample, written on the path from the root of $T$ to $v$, is $H$-realizable.
elements.sent-2411-063: The Littlestone dimension of a class $H$, denoted by $\ldim(H)$, is the minimal $d\ge 0$ such that in every $(d+1)$-depth Littlestone tree $T$ there exists a leaf which is not $H$-realizable.
elements.sent-2411-064: The effective Littlestone dimension of a class $H$, denoted by $\eldim(H)$, is the minimal $d\ge 0$ for which there exists a total Turing machine that, given as input a Littlestone tree of depth $d+1$, outputs some leaf of this tree which is not $H$-realizable.
elements.sent-2411-065: Unions of cylinders induce on $\{0,1\}^\mathbb{N}$ a topology, homeomorphic to the Cantor space.
elements.sent-2411-066: Cylinders are clopen in this topology.
elements.sent-2411-067: We use a well-known fact that the Cantor space is compact.
elements.sent-2411-068: A subset of $\{0,1\}^\mathbb{N}$ is effectively open if it is a union of an enumerable set of cylinders.
elements.sent-2411-069: A subset of $\{0,1\}^\mathbb{N}$ is effectively closed if the complement to it is effectively open.
elements.sent-2411-070: Then the set of cylinders $C$ that are subsets of $X$ is enumerable.. \end{prop.}
elements.sent-2411-071: There exists a computable enumeration $\{C_n\}_{n = 1 ^\infty$ of cylinders such that $X = \bigcup_{n = 1 ^\infty C_n$.}}
elements.sent-2411-072: We enumerate all cylinders $C$ for which there exists $N\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $C\subseteq \bigcup_{n = 1 ^N C_n$.}
elements.sent-2411-073: By compactness, since every cylinder is closed, in this way we will enumerate all cylinders $C$ such that $C\subseteq \bigcup_{n = 1 ^\infty C_n$.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Let $t \in \mathbb{N}$ and $(x_1, \ldots, x_t)\in\mathbb{N ^t$ be a sequence of $t$ natural numbers.}
elements.sent-2411-075: For $i = 1,\ldots, t$, the $i$th threshold on $(x_1, \ldots, x_t)$ is a sample: \begin{equation}(x_1,0)\ldots (x_{i-1}, 0) (x_i,1)\ldots (x_t, 1).\end{equation}. .
elements.sent-2411-076: The threshold dimension of a hypothesis class $H$, denoted by $\tdim(H)$, is the largest natural number $t$ for which there exists a sequence $(x_1, \ldots, x_t)\in\mathbb{N ^{t}$ such that for all $i = 1, \ldots, t$, the $i$th threshold on $(x_1, \ldots, x_t)$ is $H$-realizable.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Shelah~\cite{Shelah have shown that a class has finite Lilttlestone dimension if and only it has finite threshold dimension.}
elements.sent-2411-078: Hodges~\cite{hodges1997shorter and Alon et al.~\cite{Alon-et-al have shown the following quantitive version of the Shelah's result.}}
elements.sent-2411-079: Here, the effective threshold dimension of $H$, denoted by $\etdim(H)$ is the minimal $t \ge 0$ for which there exists a total Turing machine $w$ which, having on input a sequence $(x_1,\ldots, x_{t+1 )\in\mathbb{N ^{t + 1}$, outputs some $i\in\{1, \ldots, t + 1\}$ such that the $i$th threshold on $(x_1, \ldots, x_{t + 1 )$ is not $H$-realizable.}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: In fact, we show that any upper bound on the Littlestone dimension by the threshold dimension, and vice versa, extends to the effective versions of these dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-081: More precisely, the following theorem holds.. \begin{thm.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Let $H$ be a class of effective Littlestone dimension $d$.
elements.sent-2411-084: We show that its effective threshold Littlestone dimension is at most $t_d$.
elements.sent-2411-085: We have a total turing machine $A$ that, given a Littlestone tree $T$ of depth $d +1$, outputs a leaf of $T$ which is not $H$-realizable.
elements.sent-2411-086: We convert $A$ into a Turing machine $w$ that, given a sequence $\overline{x = (x_1,\ldots, x_{t_d + 1 )\in\mathbb{N ^{t_d + 1}$, outputs some $i \in\{1, \ldots, t_d + 1\}$ such that the $i$th threshold on $\overline{x}$ is not $H$-realizable.}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: The machine $w$ starts by calculating a list $T_1,\ldots, T_m$ of all Littlestone trees of depth $d +1$ where node labels are taken from the set $\{x_1,\ldots, x_{t_d + 1}\}$.
elements.sent-2411-088: Then the machine runs $A$ on all of these Littlestone trees.
elements.sent-2411-089: Each time $A$ outputs a leaf in some of these trees, it writes down the sample, written on the path to this leaf.
elements.sent-2411-090: Let $S_1, \ldots, S_m$ be the resulting list of samples.
elements.sent-2411-091: Observe that, by definition of $A$, all these samples are not $H$-realizable.
elements.sent-2411-092: The machine proceeds by constructing a set $\widehat{H}$ of functions $g\colon\{x_1, \ldots, x_{t_d + 1}\}\to\{0,1\}$ such that $g$ is not consistent with $S_\ell$ for all $\ell = 1, \ldots, m$.
elements.sent-2411-093: That set includes all restrictions of $f\in H$ to the set $\{x_1, \ldots, x_{t_d + 1}\}$.
elements.sent-2411-094: Finally, the machine goes through all $i = 1,\ldots, t_{d +1$, checking, whether the $i$th threshold on $(x_1,\ldots, x_{t_d + 1 )$ is $\widehat{H}$-realizable, brute-forcing all functions in $\widehat{H}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-095: Whenever it finds a not $\widehat{H}$-realizable threshold, which is also automatically not $H$-realizable, the machine outputs the corresponding $i$.
elements.sent-2411-096: Such threshold exists because the threshold dimension of $\widehat{H}$, as of an hypothesis class over the domain $\{x_1, \ldots, x_{t_d}\}$, is at most $t_d$.
elements.sent-2411-097: Indeed, its Littlestone dimension is at most $d$ because in every Littlestone tree $T_\ell$ over the domain $\{x_1, \ldots, x_{t_d}\}$, there exists a leaf with the sample $S_\ell$ which is not $\widehat{H}$-realizable.
elements.sent-2411-098: And by definition, the threshold dimension of a class with Littlestone dimension at most $d$ cannot exceed $t_d$.
elements.sent-2411-100: Now we have to convert a Turing machine $w$ which, given a sequence $\overline{x = (x_1,\ldots, x_{t +1 )\in\mathbb{N ^{t + 1}$, outputs a threshold on $\overline{x}$ which is not $H$-realizable, into a Turing machine $A$ that, given a depth-$(d_t + 1)$ Littlestone tree $T$, outputs a leaf in $T$ which is not $H$-realizable.}}}
elements.sent-2411-101: We let $D_T$ be the set of all natural numbers, appearing in $T$.
elements.sent-2411-102: We go through all $(t +1)$-length sequences, consisting of numbers from $D_T$, run $w$ on them and construct the list of all threshold that it outputs (that are all not $H$-realizable).
elements.sent-2411-103: We then construct a set $\widehat{H}$ of functions $g\colon D_T\to\{0, 1\}$ that are inconsistent with all these thresholds, and this set includes all restrictions of function from $H$ to $D_T$.
elements.sent-2411-104: We notice that, by construction, the threshold dimension of $H$ is at most $t$, which means that its Littlestone dimension is at most $d_t$.
elements.sent-2411-105: We use this to find in $T$, which is a tree of depth $d_t +1$, a leaf which is not $\widehat{H}$-realizable.
elements.section-2411-004: Effective Littlestone dimension vs.~computable online learning
elements.sent-2411-107: If $H$ admits a total computable online learner which makes at most $d$ mistakes, then the effective Littlestone dimension of $H$ is at most $d$.. \end{prop.}
elements.sent-2411-108: Let $L$ be a total computable online learner for $H$ with at most $d$ mistakes.
elements.sent-2411-109: Given a $(d+1)$-depth Littlestone tree $T$, we find a leaf of it on which $L$ makes $d+1$ mistakes.
elements.sent-2411-110: Namely, we give $L$ the number from the root, wait for its prediction (since $L$ is total, we will receive it), go to the child which contradicts this prediction, give the number from this child, and so on.
elements.sent-2411-111: The sample on the path to this leaf cannot by $H$-realizable because $L$ makes at most $d$ mistakes on $H$-realizable samples.
elements.sent-2411-112: Main result of this section is that the converse of this proposition is false already for $d = 2$ (although, as we will see later, it is true for $d = 1$).
elements.sent-2411-113: In our construction, to make sure that $H$ has effective Littlestone dimension at most 2, we establish two things: (a) $H$ has ordinary Littlestone dimension at most 2, (b) $H$ is effectively closed.
elements.sent-2411-114: Why do (a) and (b) imply that $H$ has effective Littlestone dimension at most 2? We have to provide an algorithm that, given a depth-3 Littlestone tree $T$, gives a leaf $\ell$ of $T$ which is not $H$-realizable.
elements.sent-2411-115: Such leaf $\ell$ exists because, by (a), the ordinary Littlestone dimension of $H$ is at most 2.
elements.sent-2411-116: Out task is to find it.
elements.sent-2411-117: For a leaf $\ell$, let $S_\ell$ be the sample, written on the path to $\ell$.
elements.sent-2411-118: Let $C_\ell$ be the cylinder, induced by $S_\ell$.
elements.sent-2411-119: A leaf $\ell$ is not $H$-realizable if and only if $C_\ell$ is a subset of the complement to $H$.
elements.sent-2411-120: Since, $H$ is effectively closed by (b), the complement to it is effectively open.
elements.sent-2411-121: Hence, by Proposition \ref{prop_enum , the set of cylinders that are subsets of the complement to $H$ is enumerable.}
elements.sent-2411-122: We start enumerating them until $C_\ell$ for some leaf $\ell$ of $T$ appears in this enumeration.
elements.sent-2411-123: In our construction, we ensure effective closeness of $H$ by defining it via an enumerable set of ``local restriction''.
elements.sent-2411-124: Each local restriction is of the form ``at this (finite) set of positions, you cannot have this combination of values''.
elements.sent-2411-125: Thus, each local restriction is, formally, a complement to a cylinder.
elements.sent-2411-126: The class $H$ will consist of functions, satisfying all these restrictions.
elements.sent-2411-127: In other words, $H$ will be an intersection of an enumerable set of complements to the cylinders.
elements.sent-2411-128: Hence, the complement to $H$ will be a union of the corresponding enumerable set of cylinders, as required in the definition of an effectively closed set.
elements.sent-2411-129: Fix a computable enumeration $L_1, L_2, L_3, \ldots$ of all partial computable learners.
elements.sent-2411-130: We say that a class $H$ ``fools'' a learner $L_i$ if either.
elements.sent-2411-131: In other words, $L_i$ incorrectly predicts $f$ every time on the sequence $(x_1, x_2, x_3, \ldots)$.
elements.sent-2411-132: If $H$ fools $L_i$, then there is no $d\in\mathbb{N}$ for which $L_i$ is an online learner for $H$ with at most $d$ mistakes.
elements.sent-2411-133: Indeed, in the case of (a), $L_i$ is not a learner for $H$, and in the case of (b), for every $n$ there exists an $H$-realizable sample $S_n$ on which $L_i$ makes $n$ mistakes.
elements.sent-2411-134: We will construct $H$ that fools every $L_i$ but has Littlestone dimension 2 and is effectively closed.
elements.sent-2411-135: Let us start with a simpler task -- for every $i$, we construct an effectively closed class $\widehat{H _i$ that fools $L_i$ (but maybe not other partial computable learners).}
elements.sent-2411-136: The class $\widehat{H _i$ will have at most 2 functions.}
elements.sent-2411-137: The construction works in (potentially infinitely many) iteration.
elements.sent-2411-138: In the first iteration, we give $1$ to $L_i$ for prediction, on the empty sample, that is, we start computing $L_i(\text{empty , 1)$.}
elements.sent-2411-139: In parallel, we start listing restrictions of the form ``$f(1) = f(k)$'' for $k =2, 3,$ and so on (forbidding different values at $1$ and $k$).
elements.sent-2411-140: If $L_i$ never halts, we will list all such restrictions.
elements.sent-2411-141: As the result, $\widehat{H _i$ will consist of two constant functions.}
elements.sent-2411-142: In this case, $L_i$ is fooled by not halting for some $\widehat{H _i$-realizable sample, namely, for the empty one.}
elements.sent-2411-144: Up to this moment, we have listed restrictions ``$f(1) = f(k)$'' for $k$ up to some $k_1\in\mathbb{N}$.
elements.sent-2411-145: We now add a restriction, forbidding $f(1)$ to be $p_1$.
elements.sent-2411-146: In other words, we set $f(1) = b_1 = \lnot p_1$.
elements.sent-2411-147: With this, the first iteration ends.
elements.sent-2411-148: So far, we have achieved two things.
elements.sent-2411-149: First, functions, satisfying our current restrictions, are exactly functions with $f(1) = \ldots = f(k_1) = b_1$.
elements.sent-2411-150: Second, $L_i(\text{empty , 1)$ halts but its output is different from $b_1$.}
elements.sent-2411-151: More generally, in our construction, after $n$ iterations, for some $k_1, \ldots, k_n\ge 1$ and for some $b_1,\ldots, b_n\in\{0, 1\}$, the following requirements will be fulfilled:.
